gh-w8-f1.answer:
StudentAPI/ — your first full Spring Boot REST project. Build a Student Management REST API with MySQL persistence, layered architecture, and input validation.

Project structure (create inside StudentAPI/):
• pom.xml — spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, mysql-connector-j, spring-boot-starter-validation
• src/main/java/com/academy/student/
  - StudentApiApplication.java (@SpringBootApplication)
  - model/Student.java (@Entity, @Table)
  - repository/StudentRepository.java (extends JpaRepository<Student, Long>)
  - service/StudentService.java (@Service, business logic)
  - controller/StudentController.java (@RestController, @RequestMapping("/api/students"))
  - exception/GlobalExceptionHandler.java (@RestControllerAdvice)
• src/main/resources/application.properties — datasource, JPA ddl-auto=update, server.port=8081

Entity fields: id (Long, @GeneratedValue), name (String, @NotBlank), email (String, unique), rollNumber (String), branch (String), percentage (Double).

Endpoints to implement:
• GET /api/students — list all students (200 OK)
• GET /api/students/{id} — get one student (200 or 404)
• POST /api/students — create student (201 Created)
• PUT /api/students/{id} — full update (200 or 404)
• DELETE /api/students/{id} — delete (204 No Content)
• GET /api/students/search?branch=CS — filter by branch

Key concepts: @RestController returns JSON automatically, @PathVariable for {id}, @RequestBody for JSON input, @Valid triggers Bean Validation, ResponseEntity controls status codes, JpaRepository gives save/findById/findAll/deleteById for free.
gh-w8-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every enterprise backend starts with a CRUD API — student portals, HR systems, and admin panels all follow Controller → Service → Repository. This project proves you can wire Spring Boot + JPA + MySQL end to end.

Setup MySQL first:
CREATE DATABASE student_db;

Run (inside StudentAPI/):
mvn spring-boot:run

Or:
mvn clean package
java -jar target/student-api-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT.jar

Test with curl or Postman:
POST http://localhost:8081/api/students
Content-Type: application/json
{"name":"Riya","email":"riya@academy.com","rollNumber":"101","branch":"CS","percentage":88.5}

Push to springboot-week-1:
git add StudentAPI/
git commit -m "Add StudentAPI — CRUD REST API with JPA"
git push

Common mistakes: forgetting @RequestBody on POST, returning entity directly without 404 check, port clash if multiple APIs run on 8080 — use different ports per project.

gh-w8-f2.answer:
EmployeeAPI/ — Employee Management REST API with department filtering and salary search. Demonstrates layered architecture and custom repository query methods.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — same starters as StudentAPI
• src/main/java/com/academy/employee/
  - EmployeeApiApplication.java
  - model/Employee.java — id, firstName, lastName, email, department, salary (BigDecimal), hireDate (LocalDate)
  - model/Department.java — optional enum (IT, HR, FINANCE, SALES)
  - repository/EmployeeRepository.java — add findByDepartment, findBySalaryBetween
  - service/EmployeeService.java
  - controller/EmployeeController.java (@RequestMapping("/api/employees"))
• application.properties — server.port=8082, spring.datasource.url=jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/employee_db

Endpoints:
• GET /api/employees — all employees
• GET /api/employees/{id} — single employee
• POST /api/employees — create (201)
• PUT /api/employees/{id} — update
• DELETE /api/employees/{id} — delete (204)
• GET /api/employees/department/{dept} — filter by department
• GET /api/employees/salary?min=30000&max=80000 — salary range search
• GET /api/employees/stats/average-salary — bonus: average salary by department

Key concepts: Constructor injection (preferred over @Autowired on fields), BigDecimal for money (never float/double), Spring Data derived query methods (findByDepartment, findBySalaryBetween), separation of concerns — controller handles HTTP, service handles rules, repository handles data.
gh-w8-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: HR and payroll systems query employees by department and salary band daily. Custom repository methods replace handwritten SQL for simple filters — exactly what Spring Data JPA is built for.

Run:
cd EmployeeAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test department filter:
GET http://localhost:8082/api/employees/department/IT

Push:
git add EmployeeAPI/
git commit -m "Add EmployeeAPI — department filter and salary search"
git push

Tip: Use BigDecimal.valueOf(75000.00) in code and @Column(precision=12, scale=2) on salary to avoid rounding bugs.

gh-w8-f3.answer:
ProductAPI/ — Inventory Management REST API for products with category filtering and stock management.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation
• src/main/java/com/academy/product/
  - ProductApiApplication.java
  - model/Product.java — id, name, sku, category, price (BigDecimal), stockQuantity (int), reorderLevel (int)
  - model/Category.java — optional @Entity or enum
  - repository/ProductRepository.java — findByCategory, findByStockQuantityLessThan
  - service/ProductService.java — stock increment/decrement logic
  - controller/ProductController.java (@RequestMapping("/api/products"))
• application.properties — server.port=8083, database product_db

Endpoints:
• GET /api/products — list all
• GET /api/products/{id} — get by id
• POST /api/products — create product
• PUT /api/products/{id} — update product
• DELETE /api/products/{id} — delete
• GET /api/products/category/{category} — filter by category
• PATCH /api/products/{id}/stock — body: {"quantity": 50} to set stock
• POST /api/products/{id}/restock — body: {"amount": 10} to add stock
• GET /api/products/low-stock — bonus: products where stockQuantity <= reorderLevel

Key concepts: PATCH for partial updates vs PUT for full replacement, business validation in service layer (stock cannot go negative), @Transactional on service methods that modify data.
gh-w8-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: E-commerce warehouses track SKU, category, and stock levels in real time. A low-stock alert endpoint is how ops teams know to reorder before shelves go empty.

Run:
cd ProductAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test restock:
POST http://localhost:8083/api/products/1/restock
{"amount": 25}

Push:
git add ProductAPI/
git commit -m "Add ProductAPI — inventory CRUD with stock management"
git push

gh-w8-f4.answer:
LibraryAPI/ — Library Management REST API with books, members, borrowing, returns, and search. Introduces JPA entity relationships.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation
• src/main/java/com/academy/library/
  - LibraryApiApplication.java
  - model/Book.java — id, title, author, isbn (unique), totalCopies, availableCopies
  - model/Member.java — id, name, email, membershipDate
  - model/BorrowRecord.java — id, book (@ManyToOne), member (@ManyToOne), borrowDate, returnDate, status (BORROWED/RETURNED)
  - repository/BookRepository, MemberRepository, BorrowRecordRepository
  - service/LibraryService.java — borrow/return business rules
  - controller/BookController.java, BorrowController.java
• application.properties — server.port=8084, database library_db

Endpoints:
• GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/books — book CRUD
• GET /api/books/search?title=java — search by title (case-insensitive)
• GET/POST /api/members — member management
• POST /api/borrow — body: {"bookId":1,"memberId":2} — borrow a book
• POST /api/return/{borrowId} — return a book
• GET /api/borrow/member/{memberId} — active borrows for a member
• GET /api/borrow/overdue — bonus: borrows past due date

Key concepts: @ManyToOne for BorrowRecord → Book and Member, cascade rules (don't cascade delete books when member deleted), decrement availableCopies on borrow and increment on return, @Query for custom search: findByTitleContainingIgnoreCase.
gh-w8-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Any system with related entities — orders and line items, appointments and patients — uses the same @ManyToOne / @OneToMany patterns. Library borrow/return is a classic transaction: two tables must stay consistent.

Run:
cd LibraryAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test borrow flow:
1. POST /api/books — create a book with availableCopies=3
2. POST /api/members — create a member
3. POST /api/borrow — {"bookId":1,"memberId":1}
4. GET /api/books/1 — availableCopies should be 2

Push:
git add LibraryAPI/
git commit -m "Add LibraryAPI — books, borrow, return with JPA relationships"
git push

gh-w8-f5.answer:
HospitalAPI/ — Hospital Management REST API with patients, doctors, and appointments. Multiple related entities with JPA mappings.

Project structure:
• pom.xml — web, data-jpa, mysql, validation
• src/main/java/com/academy/hospital/
  - HospitalApiApplication.java
  - model/Patient.java — id, name, age, gender, phone, bloodGroup
  - model/Doctor.java — id, name, specialization, phone, department
  - model/Appointment.java — id, patient (@ManyToOne), doctor (@ManyToOne), appointmentDateTime, status (SCHEDULED/COMPLETED/CANCELLED), notes
  - repository/PatientRepository, DoctorRepository, AppointmentRepository
  - service/PatientService, DoctorService, AppointmentService
  - controller/PatientController, DoctorController, AppointmentController
• application.properties — server.port=8085, database hospital_db

Endpoints:
• CRUD /api/patients — patient management
• CRUD /api/doctors — doctor management
• GET /api/appointments — list all
• POST /api/appointments — schedule appointment (validate doctor not double-booked)
• GET /api/appointments/{id}
• PUT /api/appointments/{id} — update status or notes
• DELETE /api/appointments/{id} — cancel
• GET /api/appointments/patient/{patientId}
• GET /api/appointments/doctor/{doctorId}?date=2026-06-20

Key concepts: Scheduling conflict detection in service layer, LocalDateTime for appointments, @ManyToOne on both sides of Appointment, proper HTTP status — 409 Conflict if doctor already booked at that time.
gh-w8-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Hospital, clinic, and salon booking systems all prevent double-booking. Checking existing appointments before insert is a real business rule — not something the database alone handles gracefully.

Run:
cd HospitalAPI
mvn spring-boot:run

Test conflict detection:
POST two appointments for same doctor at same time — second should return 409.

Push:
git add HospitalAPI/
git commit -m "Add HospitalAPI — patients, doctors, appointments"
git push

gh-w8-f6.answer:
Postman Collection.json — a single Postman collection that tests all five Week 8 APIs.

Requirements:
1. Create a collection named "Spring Boot Week 8 — REST APIs".
2. Add folders: StudentAPI, EmployeeAPI, ProductAPI, LibraryAPI, HospitalAPI.
3. Include at least 3 requests per API (GET all, POST create, GET by id).
4. Set collection variables: studentBaseUrl=http://localhost:8081, employeeBaseUrl=http://localhost:8082, productBaseUrl=http://localhost:8083, libraryBaseUrl=http://localhost:8084, hospitalBaseUrl=http://localhost:8085.
5. Use {{studentBaseUrl}}/api/students style URLs.
6. Add sample JSON bodies for every POST/PUT request.
7. Optional: Tests tab scripts — pm.test("Status is 201", () => pm.response.to.have.status(201));
8. Export as Collection v2.1 JSON and save as Postman Collection.json at repo root.

Key concepts: Postman collections are shareable API test suites, environment/collection variables avoid hardcoding URLs, organizing by folder mirrors your project structure.
gh-w8-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Teams share Postman collections in Git so QA, frontend devs, and new hires can hit every endpoint without reading code. Exporting JSON to your repo is standard practice in backend portfolios.

No compile step — import into Postman: File → Import → Postman Collection.json.

Run all APIs first (each on its port), then Run Collection.

Push:
git add "Postman Collection.json"
git commit -m "Add Postman collection for Week 8 REST APIs"
git push

Tip: Use Pre-request Scripts to generate unique emails (Date.now()) so repeated POST tests don't fail on duplicate key errors.

gh-w8-f7.answer:
database-scripts/ — SQL scripts to create and seed all five Week 8 databases.

Folder structure:
• database-scripts/
  - 01-create-databases.sql — CREATE DATABASE for student_db, employee_db, product_db, library_db, hospital_db
  - 02-student-schema.sql — CREATE TABLE students with columns matching Student entity
  - 03-employee-schema.sql — CREATE TABLE employees
  - 04-product-schema.sql — CREATE TABLE products
  - 05-library-schema.sql — CREATE TABLE books, members, borrow_records with FK constraints
  - 06-hospital-schema.sql — CREATE TABLE patients, doctors, appointments
  - seed-data.sql — INSERT sample rows for each table (5+ rows per main table)
  - README.md — how to run scripts in MySQL Workbench or CLI

Note: Spring Boot with spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update can auto-create tables, but production teams use explicit SQL scripts for reproducibility.

Key concepts: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY constraints, UNIQUE on email/isbn, INDEX on frequently filtered columns (department, category), UTF8MB4 charset for names.
gh-w8-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: DevOps runs migration scripts in CI/CD — you can't rely on ddl-auto=update in production. Checking SQL scripts into Git documents your schema and lets teammates bootstrap databases in minutes.

Run in MySQL:
mysql -u root -p < database-scripts/01-create-databases.sql
mysql -u root -p student_db < database-scripts/02-student-schema.sql
mysql -u root -p student_db < database-scripts/seed-data.sql

Or paste into MySQL Workbench and execute.

Push:
git add database-scripts/
git commit -m "Add database scripts for Week 8 APIs"
git push

gh-w8-f8.answer:
README.md — the front page of your springboot-week-1 repository.

Include:
1. Title: # springboot-week-1
2. Description — Week 8 Spring Boot REST API projects for Prathyu Academy.
3. Prerequisites — JDK 17+, Maven 3.8+, MySQL 8+, Postman.
4. Projects table — StudentAPI (8081), EmployeeAPI (8082), ProductAPI (8083), LibraryAPI (8084), HospitalAPI (8085) with one-line descriptions.
5. How to run each project:
   cd StudentAPI && mvn spring-boot:run
6. Database setup — link to database-scripts/ folder.
7. API testing — import Postman Collection.json.
8. Architecture — brief explanation of Controller → Service → Repository.
9. Topics covered — Spring Boot, REST, JPA, MySQL, Postman, validation, HTTP status codes.
10. What you learned — 3–5 sentences in your own words.

Use Markdown: headings, tables, bullet lists, and ```bash code blocks for commands.
gh-w8-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Interviewers skim your GitHub README before cloning code. A README with run instructions, port numbers, and architecture diagram shows you can ship and document — not just copy tutorials.

No compile step — preview in IDE or on GitHub after push.

Push:
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add README — Week 8 Spring Boot portfolio overview"
git push
gh-w8-f8.code:
# springboot-week-1

Week 8 — Spring Boot Fundamentals & REST API Development for Prathyu Academy.

## Prerequisites
- JDK 17+
- Maven 3.8+
- MySQL 8+
- Postman

## Projects
| Project | Port | Description |
|---------|------|-------------|
| StudentAPI | 8081 | Student CRUD with validation |
| EmployeeAPI | 8082 | Employee management with dept/salary filters |
| ProductAPI | 8083 | Inventory with stock management |
| LibraryAPI | 8084 | Books, borrow, return |
| HospitalAPI | 8085 | Patients, doctors, appointments |

## Quick Start
```bash
cd StudentAPI
mvn spring-boot:run
```

## Database Setup
Run scripts in `database-scripts/` to create databases and seed data.

## What I Learned
This week I built five REST APIs with Spring Boot, connected them to MySQL via Spring Data JPA, and tested everything with Postman.

gh-w8-f9.answer:
API-Documentation.md — human-readable API reference for all five Week 8 projects.

Document each API with:
1. Base URL and port.
2. Endpoint table: Method | Path | Description | Request Body | Response | Status Codes.
3. Sample request/response JSON for every endpoint.
4. Error responses — 400 validation errors, 404 not found, 409 conflict (HospitalAPI).
5. Entity field descriptions — what each field means, required vs optional.
6. Relationships diagram (text or mermaid) for LibraryAPI and HospitalAPI.

Sections:
• Student API
• Employee API
• Product API
• Library API
• Hospital API
• Common HTTP Status Codes Used
• How to Test with Postman

Key concepts: API documentation is a contract between backend and frontend teams. Good docs include examples, not just endpoint lists.
gh-w8-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Frontend developers and mobile teams work from API docs — not your Java source. Companies use Swagger in production, but Markdown docs in Git are how you prove you understand REST design before adding tooling.

No compile step — commit as Markdown.

Push:
git add API-Documentation.md
git commit -m "Add API documentation for Week 8 REST APIs"
git push

Tip: Keep docs in sync with code — if you change an endpoint path, update this file in the same commit.
gh-w8-f9.code:
# API Documentation — springboot-week-1

## Student API (port 8081)
Base URL: `http://localhost:8081`

| Method | Endpoint | Description | Status |
|--------|----------|-------------|--------|
| GET | /api/students | List all students | 200 |
| GET | /api/students/{id} | Get student by ID | 200 / 404 |
| POST | /api/students | Create student | 201 |
| PUT | /api/students/{id} | Update student | 200 / 404 |
| DELETE | /api/students/{id} | Delete student | 204 / 404 |
| GET | /api/students/search?branch=CS | Filter by branch | 200 |

### POST /api/students — Request
```json
{
  "name": "Riya Sharma",
  "email": "riya@academy.com",
  "rollNumber": "CS101",
  "branch": "CS",
  "percentage": 88.5
}
```

### POST /api/students — Response (201)
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Riya Sharma",
  "email": "riya@academy.com",
  "rollNumber": "CS101",
  "branch": "CS",
  "percentage": 88.5
}
```
